coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped CQDs to enhance the power conversion efficiency of perovskite solar cells via surface passivation
coredata.dc:creator: Kırbıyık, Çisem
Toprak, Ayşegül
Başlak, Canan
Kuş, Mahmut
Ersöz, Mustafa
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen doped CQDs (N-CQDs), as a novel additive, were successfully synthesized and characterized by XPS, TEM and UV–Vis spectrometry techniques. Importantly, we introduced N-CQDs in CH3NH3PbI3 solar cells. The results indicated that nonradiative charge back recombination decreased and the trap states in the absorber layer were passivated when N-CQDs was used an additive in CH3NH3PbI3 solution. Additionally, the morphological characterization results indicated that the crystallization problems and defects on perovskite surfaces can be easily resolved by N-CQDs additive in perovskite solution. A significant improvement in power conversion efficiency at a yield of 14% is achieved by doping of 3 vol% of N-CQDs, thanks to enhancement of charge transport properties due to the morphological and electrical changes. Herein, we propose an effective and novel additive for the fabrication of high efficiency perovskite solar cell, which can be seen that this method is simple.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CQDs
Perovskite solar cells
Film coverage
Charge transport properties

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS survey spectrum and XPS deconvolutions of (b) C1s and (c) O1s and (d) N1s for N-CQDs.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of non-doped and doped MAPbI3 films on top of the PEDOT:PSS coated ITO glass.
SEM top view images of (a) non-doped MAPbI3 and (b) MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs films.
AFM images of (a) non-doped MAPbI3 and (b) MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs films.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (b) Tauc plots according to the optical absorption data to calculate the band gap of the non-doped and doped MAPbI3 films.
Current density-voltage characteristics of the best-performing devices under (a) the light and (b) the dark conditions.
EQE spectra of non-doped and MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs PSCs fabricated.
Box chart of shunt and series resistances extracted from J-V curves of a series of non-doped and MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs PSCs fabricated.
Box chart of the photovoltaic characteristics of a series of non-doped and MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs PSCs fabricated (a) Voc, (b) Jsc, (c) FF, and (d) ƞ.
Schematic illustration of preparation procedure and the device architecture of the perovskite solar cells.
Average roughness differences, statistical grain size distribution and direct bandgap transitions for non-doped and doped perovskite surfaces.
List of photovoltaic parameters extracted from illuminated J-V curves.
The dark saturation current density (Jo), ideality factor (n) shunt (Rsh) and series resistances (Rs) extracted from J-V curves.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped CQDs to enhance the power conversion efficiency of perovskite solar cells via surface passivation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Konya Technical University, 42075, Konya, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Konya Technical University, 42075, Konya, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic and Automation, Selcuk University, 42075, Konya, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electronic and Automation, Selcuk University, 42075, Konya, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Selcuk University, 42075, Konya, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Selcuk University, 42075, Konya, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen doped CQDs (N-CQDs), as a novel additive, were successfully synthesized and characterized by XPS, TEM and UV–Vis spectrometry techniques. Importantly, we introduced N-CQDs in CH3NH3PbI3 solar cells. The results indicated that nonradiative charge back recombination decreased and the trap states in the absorber layer were passivated when N-CQDs was used an additive in CH3NH3PbI3 solution. Additionally, the morphological characterization results indicated that the crystallization problems and defects on perovskite surfaces can be easily resolved by N-CQDs additive in perovskite solution. A significant improvement in power conversion efficiency at a yield of 14% is achieved by doping of 3 vol% of N-CQDs, thanks to enhancement of charge transport properties due to the morphological and electrical changes. Herein, we propose an effective and novel additive for the fabrication of high efficiency perovskite solar cell, which can be seen that this method is simple.

ce:section.ce:para: As new generation photovoltaic materials, metal halide perovskites are the most promising light harvesters due to the remarkable enhancement in their certified power conversion efficiencies (PCE) reaching from ∼4% to 25.2% [1–3]. Because of their unique optoelectronic properties e.g., high absorption coefficient, bipolar carrier transport ability, long carrier diffusion length and defect tolerance ability together with the low cost production and low-temperature solution processability, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are seemed to be highly influential in thin film photovoltaic device area [4]. Generally, comparison of photovoltaic devices is scored by fabrication cost, efficiency and lifetime. The balancing of these parameters is the most important issue to have a place of photovoltaic device in global energy market. For PSCs, although there are many advantages, further improvements will be highly beneficial for their future commercial application. The using of non-toxic materials, reducing of charge carrier recombination, industrial large-scale fabrication and enhancement of the stability under ambient conditions must be still illuminated [5].

Initially, perovskite materials were used in n-i-p FTO/mesoporous scaffold/perovskite material/hole transport layer/metal contact configuration. However, this configuration needs high temperature sintering process. Despite that, the planar PSCs (for both n-i-p or p-i-n configuration) can be manufactured by low temperature solution process. Therefore, planar PSCs are more preferable due to issues in cost effectiveness. To date, methyl ammonium lead iodide perovskite (CH3NH3PbI3-MAPbI3) based PSCs are the most investigated light absorber in terms of both planar and mesoscopic architectures [6]. The key performance limiting parameter for organohalide PSCs is the formation of defect at surface because of the iodine and methylamine ion/defect migration [7]. The weak binding and instability cause to the escaping of organic cations from perovskite crystals, leading to form non-stoichiometric surface of perovskite layer. İt results to nonradiative charge recombination on grain boundaries [8]. The nonradiative charge recombination lowers the fill factor (FF), thereby results a decreasing of the overall power output that cells can produce. Owing to non-uniformity and the presence of defects, the hysteresis in the current-voltage curves is also another problem against to commercialization [9]. Various routes have been studied to improve the crystallization of perovskite film resulting to passivation of defects such as, controlling of surface morphology using different deposition techniques (spin-coating, spray-coating, blade-coating or vacuum evaporation), introducing small organic molecules as interfacial modifier or tuning the halogen and cation composition ratios [10–13]. It has been proposed that the using non-stoichiometric ratio of PbI2 or CH3CH3I precursors supresses charge back recombination and excess volume of PbI2 improves the perovskite film crystallinity, thus, passivates the defects at the grain boundaries [14]. Also, the utilizing of Lewis bases thiophene and pyridine to treat perovskite surfaces can considerably reduce the nonradiative charge back recombination, achieving the electronic passivation of the uncoordinated Pb atoms within the perovskite crystal [15].

Additive engineering is considered to be one of most effective route to control the crystallization of perovskite layer passivating grain boundaries. Additives can be classified into seven groups: metal halide salts, organic halide salts, inorganic acids, solvents, polymers and small molecules, fullerene derivatives and nanoparticles [16–19]. Among them, small carbon nanoparticles, also known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are the new and promising member of these additives. In last decade, significant attention has been paid by many researchers in more and more research fields because of their controllable size, electrical and optical properties [20]. CQDs was used as a component in different applications, such as light-emitting diodes, sensors, solar cells, biomedicines and so on [21]. So far, the best PCEs of CQD-doped MAPbI3 solar cell have been reported to be over 19% [7,22]. Additionally, it has been claimed that this nanomaterial shows high water and thermal stability, which make them useful for large scale production [23].

In this report, we introduce a simple method to increase the efficiency of planar p-i-n based PSCs using nitrogen doped CQDs (N-CQDs) as a novel additive to the best of our knowledge. N-CQDs is incorporated into MAPbI3 solution to control the crystallization process helping to retard the aggregation of perovskite crystals. N-CQDs addition may help to form smoother perovskite film and to reduce the defects or voids between perovskite domains. It is worth noting that the carbonyl and amine functional groups not only passivates the trap states but also show a strong effect on uniform size distribution since these groups can coordinate with Pb in perovskite [24,25]. Additionally, the interaction between uncoordinated iodine ions and N–H groups could improve the charge carrier extraction on perovskite active layer. Due to the coordination ability of N–H component of N-CQDs could help to effective passivation of MAPbI3 [26]. As the results, we achieved a maximum efficiency of 14% with a high current density (Jsc) of 23.1 mA cm−2 and an open circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.822 V. In comparison to control device, an ordered crystallization was observed onto PEDOT:PSS films, which mainly provides better charge transfer properties after the charge separation. Therefore, the FF showed a ∼100% improvement (from 31% to 59%) after optimizing the percentage volume of N-CQDs additive.

Kombucha tea fungus and black tea were purchased. All other chemicals were commercially available and used as pure grade without further purification. Ethylene diamine (≥99%, EDA), and filter papers were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Milli-Q water was used in all synthesis and spectroscopic measurements. The fermented tea was used as the carbon resource to synthesize N-CQDs. The fermented tea with kombucha mushroom was kept in fermentation for 20 days. After 20 days, the tea was filtered and separated from the fungus. 0.2 mL of EDA was added to the fermented tea. The mixture solution is stirred vigorously for approximately 5 h. The green emission indicates that particles are formed. The N-CQDs solution was filtered by a 0.2 μm porous size filter. Unreacted chemicals were removed by vacuum evaporating and the mixture was allowed to dry overnight in the oven. Appropriate amounts of N-CQDs were taken and their solutions with water were prepared and used for characterization [27,28].

ITO coated glasses with a sheet resistance of 10 Ω sq−1 (1.5 × 1.5 cm2) were used as substrates. Firstly, ITO coated glasses were etched with hydrochloric acid solution. The patterned ITO glasses were cleaned with hellmanex, acetone, isopropanol and deionized water for 15 min with the aid of an ultrasonic cleaner. At the end of the cleaning process, the glasses were dried by spraying N2 gas. Later oxygen plasma system was used to activate the glass surfaces and remove organic impurities. Onto ITO glasses ready for coating, PEDOT: PSS was deposited by spin coating for 1 min at 2000 rpm and annealed at 120 °C for 10 min. To prepare the perovskite solution, PbI2 (1.23 M) and CH3NH3I (1.54 M) were dissolved in 2.5 mL γ- butyrolactone (GBL) and stirred with the magnetic stirrer at 70 °C for 24 h. N-CQDs were dissolved in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) at concentration of 10 mg/mL. Then different volume percentage of N-CQDs (0%, 1%, 3% and 5%) were added into the MAPbI3 solution. After that, perovskite solution or CDs-doped perovskite solution was deposited on the PEDOT: PSS layer at 6000 rpm for 50 s. About 35 s after the initiation of spin coating, toluen (80 μL) was dropped onto the substrates to obtain a uniform film and then annealed at 110 °C for 30 min. 40 mg of PCBM were dissolved in chlorobenzene and chloroform for use as electron transfer material. Afterward PCBM solution was spin coated at 2000 rpm for 60 s and annealed 110 °C for 30 min. Finally, 60 nm aluminium top electrode was thermally evaporated at 1 × 10−6 mbar. The active area of final solar cells was calculated to be 0.07 cm2.

Optical absorption analyses of N-CQDs were carried out by PerkinElmer Lambda 25 UV–Vis spectrophotometer and PerkinElmer LS 55luminescence spectrometer. The particle scanning measurements were performed on a JEOL JEM 2100 FHRTEM brand Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer. The current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics of the PSCs were measured under illumination and dark conditions using a Keithley 2400 source meter with glove-box integrated ATLAS solar simulator under AM 1.5 condition. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were performed by using Bruker Advance D8 instrument with Cu Kα radiation at a scan rate of 2° min−1 for 2θ between 10° and 55°. The prepared film thicknesses were measured by a contact profilometer (NanoMap-500LS 3D Stylus). The morphological changes and the coverage of the perovskite films were characterized by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Evo) and atomic force microscope (AFM, NT-MDT AFM NTEGRA Solaris). To determine the statistical grain size distribution of perovskite films, AFM images were characterized by a software processing images equipped with AFM. This software uses an algorithm for the quantitative evaluation of the images to calculate the particles and volumes automatically onto selected area and provides an average grain size distribution. Optical absorption analyses of perovskite thin films were carried out by Shimadzu UVmini-1240 ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrometer. The external quantum efficiencies (EQE) were analysed as a function of wavelength by a photomodulation spectroscopy setup with a 300 W xenon light source. A Si photodiode was used to calibrate the power density of the monochromatic light.

For the structural analysis of N-CQDs, XPS method was used. The survey spectrum is given in Fig. 1 (a) . The strong peaks at 531.1 eV and 285.1 eV correspond to binding energies of O1s and C1s, respectively [29]. As seen from the high resolution spectrum of C1s in Fig. 1 (b), four peaks at 287.0, 285.1 and 283.7 eV can be attributed to C–O, C–N and C–C bonds, respectively [30]. As shown in Fig. 1 (c), the peak of O1s at 531.4 eV and 530.1 eV can be assigned to C–O–C and CO bonds, respectively [31]. In Fig. 1 (d), the peaks located at 400.3 eV, 399.0 eV and 398.2 eV corresponds to C–N, N–H and C–NC bonds, respectively, which confirms that CQDs were successfully functionalized by –NH2 group [32]. For further structural analysis, TEM images and UV–Vis absorption measurements were recorded (Figs. S1 and S2, respectively). The TEM images (Fig. S1) show the uniform dispersion of N-CQDs in DMSO solution. The average diameter of N-CQDs were mainly calculated to be in the range of 2–5 nm. Similarly, the well-dispersed N-CQDs solution was analysed by UV lamp, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrometer. The fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs were acquired for excitation wavelength at 360 nm in the aqueous dispersion of N-CQDs, as seen in Fig. S2. N-CQDs solution exhibits bright blue-green fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp (in inset Fig. S2). The emission peak at around 490 nm is symmetrical and has a very high emission intensity. In addition, a strong absorption peak at around 320 nm was observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. This attributed to the n–p∗ transition belonging to the CO groups on the surface of N-CQDs [33]. N-CQDs were found to be stable even after 60 days and more than 60 days in the fluorescence intensity of stock.

A p-i-n planar structure was adopted for the fabrication of PSC, which is ITO/PEDOT:PSS (50 nm)/MAPbI3 (90 nm)/PCBM (50 nm)/Al (60 nm), as given in Scheme 1 . We used XRD technique to examine the effect of N-CQDs additive on the crystallization of the MAPbI3/PEDOT:PSS/ITO surfaces (represented in Fig. 2 ). The XRD patterns of all surfaces consist of eight main peaks at 14.4°, 20.4°, 23.9°, 28.8°, 32.3°, 41.0°, 43.6° and 51.0°, which are corresponding to (110), (200), (211), (220), (310), (224), (314) and (404) for the MAPbI3, respectively. Additionally, the presence of PEDOT:PSS and PbI2 phases can be determined in XRD spectra at 21.8°, 30.7° and 35.7° and 24.9°, respectively. The perovskite lattice is coherent with the findings reported in the literature [34]. It can be seen that the MAPbI3 films with N-CQDs additive show a significant affect the crystal structure. The peak intensities increased with the increasing volume of N-CQDs additive. Notably, the increased peak intensities of the (110) planes suggest that the N-CQDs additive leads to a better crystallization [35]. To confirm that, crystal sizes of non-doped MAPbI3 films were calculated from XRD patterns by using Scherrer formula, which is a well-known method. The Scherrer formula was applied to (110) peak for all MAPbI3 film and full width at half maximum (FWHM) values of these peaks were calculated to be 0.146, 0.0589, 0.0579 and 0.0508 for non-doped and doped with 1, 3 and 5 vol% of N-CQDs, respectively. Crystal size of non-doped MAPbI3 film was calculated to be 54.8 nm, whereas crystal size of doped MAPbI3 films were calculated to be 135.8, 138.2 and 157.8 nm after doping with 1, 3 and 5 vol% of N-CQDs, respectively. As reported, N-CQDs could act like the nucleation centre to enhance the crystallinity of MAPbI3, which could lead to an increase in grain size of crystals [22]. These results are in a well agreement with the findings of morphological features. Fig. 3 (a and b) present SEM images of the top surfaces of the ITO/PEDOT:PSS/MAPbI3 (non-doped) and ITO/PEDOT:PSS/MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs, respectively. In addition to more compact surface and fully coverage observed, reduced gaps between crystal grains than that of non-doped MAPbI3 surface. It is obviously seen that the surfaces show a fewer density of pinholes by doping of each volume of N-CQDs into MAPbI3 solutions [see Fig. S3 (a-b) for the SEM images of MAPbI3-1% N-CQDs and MAPbI3-5% N-CQDs, respectively]. The smoother surfaces could be attributed to the presence of carbonyl and amine functional groups on N-CQDs acting like an intermediate for Pb2+ ions [36]. To confirm this hypothesis, the AFM images, the average surface roughness (Ra) and grain size distribution of ITO/PEDOT:PSS/MAPbI3 were examined. In Fig. 4 (a and b) and Fig. S4 (a-b), it can be seen that the non-doped MAPbI3 film have lots of gaps among the grains with the size of ∼50 nm. Even with addition of a small volume of N-CQDs [1 vol% N-CQDs, see in Fig. S4 (a)], more continuous perovskite crystal formation and more compact films were obtained. The films prepared using N-CQDs additive covers the majority of PEDOT:PSS surfaces. As seen in three-dimensional (3D) images and Table 1 , the Ra values decreased from 14.5 nm (non-doped) to 10.7 nm (MAPbI3-1% N-CQDs) showing the improved surface to form a better upper layer growth. Although the Ra values of MAPbI3 film showed an increasing from 10.7 nm to 13.5 nm with the increasing volume of N-CQDs additive, one can observe that the films prepared using proper volume of additive improve the surface morphological properties. The determined grain sizes of the non-doped and doped MAPbI3 films (Table 1) indicates that the N-CQDs additive seems to have a strong effect on the grain size distribution. This more ordered perovskite layer leads to form smaller gaps between the grains than that of non-doped MAPbI3 films, which is coherent with the SEM images.

The effect of the presence of N-CQDs in MAPbI3 films on the optical properties was investigated using UV–Vis absorption data. The absorption spectra of the non-doped and doped MAPbI3 films are shown in Fig. 5 (a). All MAPbI3 films showed the characteristic strong absorption around ∼750–775 nm indicating to the optical band gap of 1.5–1.6 eV. The broad absorbance in the range from 500 to 350 could be related to presence of PbI2 [37]. This is also usual for the UV–Vis spectra of MAPbI3 films. It can be seen that N-CQDs additive decreased the optical absorption. It could be attributed to the dilution of perovskite solution after the N-CQDs addition. As mentioned in experimental section, N-CQDs were dissolved in DMSO. Therefore, the optical absorption decreased with the increasing volume of N-CQDs additive since each addition will cause a decrease in final MAPbI3 solution concentration. It is obvious that the N-CQDs mainly absorb ultraviolet light [38]. In comparison to non-doped surface, N-CQDs doping creates a small blue shift in absorption spectra at 300–400 nm due to the n-to-π∗ transition of the carbony functional groups [39]. In addition to that, it is known that the particle size of materials doped has an effect on the optical properties of thin films obtained. Even though the theoretical approach needs still to be improved, such a blue-shift is considered to be a function of decreased particle sizes of thin films due to the radiative depolarization effects. Also, more homogeneous crystal grains could contribute to this shift [40]. Therefore, the presence of the N-CQDs in MAPbI3 films has a strong effect on the optical band gap of MAPbI3 much. The optical band gaps of non-doped and doped MAPbI3 films can be determined by fitting of the absorbance data to Tauc plot of (hνα)2 vs. (hν), where α is the absorption coefficient [41]. α is calculated by the following equation [42]:
(1)
α = −
(
1 d
)
ln
[
T
(
1 − R
)
2
)
]
where d is the films thickness, T is transmittance and R is reflectance of films. The relationship between optical band gaps and the volume of N-CQDs additive is given in Fig. 5(b) and Table 1. The direct band gap of non-doped MAPbI3 film was calculated to be 1.593 eV. After the addition of 1 vol% of N-CQDs, the direct band gap values increased to 1.614 eV. The direct band gap values decreased to 1.608 and 1.600 eV with the increasing of volume of N-CQDs additive. This work indicates that N-CQDs additive can be utilized to engineer the direct band gap of MAPbI3.

In order to identify the photovoltaic characteristics of devices produced, J-V curve were recorded under illumination and dark conditions and are shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b), respectively. The corresponding device parameters are listed in Table 2 . The control device based on non-doped MAPbI3 absorber layer shows a Voc of 0.936 V, Jsc of 15.18 mA cm−2, a FF of 46.96%, yielding a PCE of 8.3%. The optimized device based on MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs absorber layer results a Voc of 0.822 V, Jsc of 23.07 mA cm−2, a FF of 58.79%, generating a PCE of 13.9%. The Jsc values show an order Jsc (3% N-CQDs additive) > Jsc (1% N-CQDs additive) > Jsc (5% N-CQDs additive) > Jsc (non-doped). The improved Jsc values after N-CQDs addition could be explained by improved charge extraction ability of perovskite layer [43]. Even though, the doped MAPbI3 PSCs results similar Jsc values, the small difference between MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs and MAPbI3-5% N-CQDs could be ascribed to the poor morphology with further increasing the volume of N-CQDs additive to 5%. Although, interfacial properties and energy band bap matching were improved, the Voc values interestingly decreased after N-CQDs addition. This could be related to scan rate or speed used to obtain J-V curves. According to literature, different scan rates or speeds may cause to different Jsc or Voc values due to the hysteresis of PSCs, which is a common problem encountered. Since our study aimed to improve PCE and reproducibility of MAPbI3 PSCs, this was not discussed here. As can be seen, the FF values display a significant increasing from 47% to 59% in an obvious trend to be FF(3% N-CQDs additive) > FF(5% N-CQDs additive) > FF(1% N-CQDs additive) > FF(non-doped). This improvement is the key parameter in the increasing of overall PCE. The passivation of the trap states in the absorber layer leads to reduce the nonradiative charge recombination, which is indicative of improved FF values [44]. Additionally, the larger improvement in values of FF could be attributed to good ohmic contacts, enhanced free charge generation and extraction efficiency. For a typical inverted PSC, the yielded efficiencies are little different from efficiencies reported in literature. These could be attributed to the differences between the film thicknesses or coating methods used in this study and reported ones. Particularly, thickness of hole transport layer effects the reaching of light to the absorber perovskite layer and have a strong impact resistance. The thickness of hole transport layer could improve or compromise the charge carrier generation [45].

The EQE spectra of non-doped and MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs devices are given in Fig. 7 . The non-doped PSC resulted a EQE value of 70%, whereas MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs PSC resulted a high EQE value of 77% at 400–500 nm. The increase in EQE value matches with findings of J-V characteristics. Since CQDs show energy down shifting properties, the light at shorter wavelengths is converted to blue-green light in this study [46]. This provides an increase in EQE at shorter wavelengths, which results an enhancement in device efficiency.

To identify the carrier transport and recombination properties, we analysed the dark J-V curves using Shockley-Read-Hall equation [47], as seen in below,
(2)
J =
J o
(
e x p
(
q V
n
K
T
)
− 1
)
where J is the dark current density, Jo is the dark saturation current density, q is the elementary charge, V is the applied voltage, K is the Boltzmann constant, n is the ideality factor and T is the absolute temperature. The calculated Jo and n can be seen in Table 3 . It is obvious that the Jo values decreased remarkably from 1.99 × 10−3 to 4.43 × 10−5. It could be explained by the reduced nonradiative recombination losses after the N-CQDs addition, which agrees with the photovoltaic characteristics. As seen in Table 3, the ideality factor decreased from 3.99 for the control device to 3.62, 3.18 and 3.31 for the MAPbI3-1% N-CQDs, MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs and MAPbI3-5% N-CQDs devices, respectively. It indicates that the interfacial charge extraction properties were improved and the nonradiative recombination due to defects on the absorber layer were suppressed [48]. Still, one can see that high n values were obtained for non-doped and doped MAPbI3 PSCs. These high values of n can be the first hint suggesting that the bulk recombination occurs in perovskite layer [49]. Also, high values of n could be possibly attributed to non-capacitive effects consisting of series resistances, which is mentioned below.

The improved FF values strongly depend on the series resistance (Rs) of solar cells produced [50]. One can extract the Rs values from the J-V curves. The lowest Rs value was obtained to be 2.85 Ω cm2 for the MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs compared with the other non-doped and doped solar cells and Rs values reduced from 6.29 Ω cm2 for control device to 3.58 Ω cm2 and 2.97 Ω cm2 for MAPbI3-1% N-CQDs and MAPbI3-5% N-CQDs devices, respectively. These results perfectly match with the findings from photovoltaic properties. It supports that the origin in FF losses may be ascribed to non-uniformity of perovskite layer without N-CQDs additive [51]. It can be observed that the shunt resistance (Rsh) clearly increased from 150.8 Ω cm2 for control device to 756.4 Ω cm2 for the MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs device. This also matches with photovoltaic characteristics. Additionally, the statistic charts of Rsh and Rs values extracted from J-V curves of control device and MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs device were given in Fig. 8 .

20 devices also were produced to show the effect of N-CQDs additive in MAPbI3 layer on the reproducibility of PSCs. The statistic charts of photovoltaic characteristics for non-doped (control device) and MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs (champion device) is given in Fig. 9 . The results show similar tendency to the best yielded devices. As expected, major improvement of the FF is achieved for MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs devices, which agrees with the best device results. Highly ordered perovskite surface for MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs devices leads to an increase the reproducibility of FF values. The highest FF value for MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs device is obtained to be 65.2%, which is a significant improve to compare with 47.1% FF of non-doped device.

In summary, N-CQDs were successfully synthesized using EDA. N-CQDs additive was introduced into perovskite absorber layer via doping of perovskite pre-mixed solution. With the interaction of uncoordinated Pb atoms within the perovskite crystal, N-CQDs act like an intermediate and help to form dense and smooth perovskite layer. Additionally, the carbonyl and amine functional groups on N-CQDs effectively passivates the trap states and decreased the nonradiative charge recombination in the MAPbI3 layer. An appropriate volume of additive in the perovskite layer presents a strong effect on the solar cell efficiencies fabricated. The MAPbI3-3% N-CQDs device yielded a PCE of 14%. Our results showed that the N-CQDs additive is important to coat a hole-free and smother absorber layer for efficient PSCs.

Çisem Kırbıyık: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Investigation. Ayşegül Toprak: Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Canan Başlak: Methodology, Writing - original draft. Mahmut Kuş: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation. Mustafa Ersöz: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Validation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank to financial support of Selcuk University Scientific Research Council (PN: 19101003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2020.154897.
